title: Stable Diffusion Magic: Effortlessly Swap Backgrounds and Hair Colors in Your Portraits
description:
Welcome to our latest tutorial on "Stable Diffusion Magic: Effortlessly Swap Backgrounds and Hair Colors in Your Portraits"! Discover the power of image transformation using the advanced Juggernaut XL version 7 with Stable Diffusion. This tutorial is perfect for anyone looking to creatively modify portraits with ease.

🔍 What You'll Learn:

- Setting Up: Learn how to install and set up the necessary extensions for Stable Diffusion on Windows.
- Person Mask Extension: We'll guide you through using the 'Person Mask' extension to focus edits on specific areas of your portrait.
- Background Changes: Watch as we transform backgrounds, ranging from simple colors to complex scenes like Egyptian pyramids and Christmas settings.
- Hair Color Modifications: Step-by-step process to change hair colors, including tips for achieving the most realistic look.
- Troubleshooting: We'll cover common issues like masking imperfections and how to overcome them for optimal results.

📌 Important Tips:

- Ensu
transcript: Imagine changing your photos backdrop and hair color with just a few clicks.  Let's explore this exciting journey with stable diffusion.  I am using the Juggernaut XL version 7 checkpoint  with the automatic 1111 stable diffusion installed on windows.  Before we start, you need to install an extension.  Go to the Extensions tab and click on Available.  Then press the Load From button.  Here you can find all the extensions available for stable diffusion.  Now search for Person Mask and install this extension by pressing the Install button.  In my case, it says Installed.  Then you will have to close and reopen stable diffusion.  We will use this extension in the Image to Image tab.  I am uploading a portrait photo since this extension only works with persons.  Here you can set the settings you like.  I am using 30 sampling steps.  For the size, use the same ratio as your input image,  which in my case is 1024 pixels.  The CFG scale is set to 5 and the Denoise strength is set to 1.  Click on a Person Mask Generator extension and check Enable.  As you can see, it creates a mask right away.  I will remove the face mask and choose from the list for the background.  As you can see now, the background around the person is masked.  I use a prompt like simple orange background blurred.  Then I hit Generate to get a new image with the background changed.  As you can see, it did a good job in this case.  Now I will try an Egyptian Pyramid background.  In this case, the Pyramid is too clear.  I want it to be a little more blurred.  So I add Blurred to the prompt.  Yes, now it looks more realistic.  Now I am doing one with a Christmas background.  This one came out pretty nice as well.  Now let's try another mask for clothes.  I am deleting the background and adding clothes.  I am trying to change it to a black sweater.  And as you can see, it failed.  So this extension is not perfect.  I tried another seed to see if I can get a better version.  Now it's better, but you can still see a red contour around.  Let's try another mask.  One for the hair.  I will try to add purple hair with a denoise strength of 0.5.  The result is pretty realistic.  However, if I want the hair to have a stronger purple hue,  I use a denoise strength of 0.75 to see how it changes the generation.  This setting yields more purple in the hair,  but it starts to create some edges around the hair.  I think this is because the masking isn't perfect, resulting in that edge.  Using a denoise strength of 1 results in an even stronger purple,  but the masking problem becomes more pronounced,  though it can probably be fixed in Photoshop.  If you go to the extension and enable override mask settings,  you will find some extra settings to experiment with.  Changing the mask blur to 64 seems to fix the strange edge,  making it look natural, but not all the hair turns purple.  Therefore, you have to fine-tune the combination of denoise,  strength, and mask.  I am trying one with a mask blur of 0.45.  You can also experiment with other colors like green,  blonde, or pink hair.  I suggest trying different seeds and settings until you find something that works for you.  White hair is another option, but its effectiveness depends on the photo and the quality of the mask.  In some portraits, it does a good job, in others not so much.  There are various types of masks you can choose from,  and you can combine them to select exactly what you need from the photo.  Add and remove them to fit your needs.  That's all for today. Thank you for watching.  If you want to see more videos related to stable diffusion,  please like and subscribe to the Pixeroma channel.  Have a great day.
